question: Christianity spread fast in Nigeria because it
solution:
The history of Christianity in Nigeria can be traced back to the 15th century, when the Portuguese were the first Europeans to arrive the shores of the region via the Atlantic.  The Portuguese brought Christianity with them but were unable to successfully plant Christianity because of their involvement in slave trade. It can be argued that the actual intent behind their voyage was more in the interest of slave business, than it was for missionary goals and objectives. Most of the Portuguese slave traders took Nigerian slaves to be resold in the Americas and parts of Europe. Hence, they were not committed to missionary work.

After the abolition of the slave trade in the British Empire in 1833, things took an interesting turn. Slaves captured by European masters were freed and sent to Freetown in Sierra Leone. These slaves had lived with their masters in Europe and America, and many had accepted Christianity at the time they were freed. In 1841, freed slaves arrived in Sierra Leone and gradual changes began in British policies. Slavery was frowned upon, and the British government took it upon itself to enforce new laws and policies, in a bid to stop trading and owning slaves.

Towards the end of the 19th century, some of the freed slaves began to retrace their ways back home. Freed slaves from Nigeria sailed from Sierra Leone to Lagos and Badagry on vessels acquired from slave courts in Freetown. Some of the freed slaves settled in Abeokuta (capital of Ogun State) and other cities in western Nigeria. They also had opportunities to acquire education offered by the Church Missionary Society (CMS). They practiced Christianity and preached the gospel to their family and kinsmen.  Over time, a good number of former slaves converted to Christianity. One of those slaves worthy of mention was Samuel Ajayi Crowther. He was captured at age 12 by Fulani slave raiders and sold to Portuguese slave traders. He later regained his freedom and went on to become the first African to be ordained bishop by the Christian Missionary Society (CMS) in addition to his consecration as bishop of the Niger territory. Samuel was one of the pioneers of local Christian missionary work in Nigeria and contributed to the translation of the Bible into Yoruba language.

subject: History

question: How many geo-political zones are there in Nigeria?
solution: The states in Nigeria are grouped into six geopolitical zones, the North Central (NC), North East (NE), North West (NW), South West (SW), South East (SE) and South (SS).
subject: History

question: Who established the Sokoto Caliphate?
solution: The Sokoto Caliphate was a West African Empire that became a part of northern Nigeria. It was founded by the charismatic Fulani Islamic scholar and political leader Usman dan Fodio upon his conquer of the Hausa people
subject: History

question: Which constitution laid the foundation for the establishment of political parties in Nigeria?
solution: The Clifford's constitution of 1922 introduced Elective principles which laid the foundation for the establishment of political parties in Nigeria.
subject: History

question: The _____ conference of 1950 discussed the provisions of the Macpherson's constitution.
solution: The Ibadan Conference of 1950 was convened to discuss the provisions of the Macpherson Constitution.
subject: History

question: In what year was Lagos annexed by the British?
solution: Lagos Colony was a British colonial possession centred on the port of Lagos in what is now southern Nigeria. Lagos was annexed on 6 August 1861 under the threat of force by Commander Beddingfield of HMS Prometheus who was accompanied by the Acting British Consul, William McCoskry.
subject: History

question: What was the first capital of the Kanem-Borno empire?
solution: Kanem-Bornu was probably founded around the mid-9th century, and its first capital was at Njimi, northeast of Lake Chad.
subject: History

question: After the death of Said bin Sultan in 1856, the Asian section of the empire was ruled by ________.
solution: After the death of Said bin Sultan in 1856 the empire was divided between his sons into two sultanates, an African section (Sultanate of Zanzibar) ruled by Majid bin Said and an Asian section (Sultanate of Muscat and Oman) ruled by Thuwaini bin Said.
subject: History

question: When did the Madhist war begin?
solution: The Mahdist War was a war between the Mahdist Sudanese of the religious leader Muhammad Ahmad bin Abd Allah, who had proclaimed himself the "Mahdi" of Islam, and the forces of the Khedivate of Egypt, initially, and later the forces of Britain.
subject: History

question: Nigeria joined UNO as the ___ member.
solution: Nigeria became the 99th member of the United Nations General Assembly, eight days after her independence in 1960.
subject: History

question: Who was the first state president of the republic of South Africa?
solution: The Republic of South Africa was proclaimed on 21 May 1961. Charles Robberts Swart, the last Governor-General, was sworn in as the first State President. The title 'State President' was originally used for the head of state of the Boer Republics, and like them, the holder of the office wore a sash with the Republic's coat of arms. He was elected to a seven-year term by the Parliament of South Africa, and was not eligible for re-election.
subject: History

question: The Trans-Atlantic slave trade was abolished in Britain in what year?
solution: Britain finally abolished the trans-Atlantic slave trade in 1807, and the United States implemented its ban a year later in 1808. Despite these legal bans, and subsequent acts to suppress the trade in the United States and elsewhere, the illegal trans-Atlantic slave trade continued into the 1860s.
subject: History

question: When was the Census crisis in Nigeria?
solution: The 1962 population census put the total population of the country at 45 million, out of which the south had 24 million and the north 21 million. This was the first time the population of the south would exceed that of the north. The population count created a big political problem for the Balewa government, which had to cancel it and order a re-count.
subject: History

question: When did Christianity start in Sierra Leone?
solution: Christianity was first introduced in Sierra Leone in 1792, however, Sierra Leone is a predominately Muslim country today. Seventy-eight percent of the population are Muslim, and the country's two largest ethnic groups, the Mende and Temne, are Muslim majority.
subject: History

question: Islam came to Nigeria in the ______ century.
solution: The spread of Islam in Nigeria dates back to the eleventh century when it first appeared in Borno in the northeast of the country. Later Islam emerged in Hausaland in the northwest and its influence was evident in Kano and Katsina.
subject: History

question: The first school in Nigeria was __________.
solution: The first primary school in Nigeria was founded in 1843 in the town of Badagry. The school was named the Nursery of Infant Church which was later renamed as St. Thomas Anglican Nursery and primary school by Rev. Golmer of the Church Missionary Society (CMS).
subject: History

question: Nigeria's vice president in the second republic was
solution: Ekwueme was the first elected Vice President of Nigeria from 1979 to 1983 during the Second Nigerian Republic serving under President Shehu Shagari as a member of the National People's Party (NPN).
subject: History

question: The economic reforms of Theodore II of Ethiopia involved which of the following?
subject: History

question: Which of these was not an effect of the Great Trek?
solution: The Great Trek led directly to the founding of several autonomous Boer republics, namely the South African Republic (also known simply as the Transvaal), the Orange Free State, and the Natalia Republic. As a result of the Trek, the Afrikaners remained politically divided for many years. Furthermore, the Trek resulted in the cultural and economic isolation of the Boers. The Great Trek increased the conflicts between the Boers and indigenous tribes, but, on the other hand stimulated trade between black and white groups.

options.a: Afrikaners remained politically divided for many years
options.b: The Trek resulted in the cultural and economic isolation of the Boers.
options.c: The Great Trek led directly to the founding of several autonomous Boer republics
options.d: It reduced trade between black and white groups

subject: History

question: Clothe dyeing started in what part of Nigeria?
solution: Adire textile is a resist-dyed cloth produced and worn primarily by the Yoruba people of southwestern Nigeria.
subject: History

question: How many states did General Murtala Mohammed create?
solution: On February 3, 1976, the Military Government of Murtala Muhammed created new states and renamed others, the states he created include: Bauchi, Benue, Borno, Imo, Niger, Ogun, and Ondo. This brought the total number of states in Nigeria to nineteen in 1976.
subject: History

question: In what year was the Oil River protectorate established?
solution: The Oil Rivers Protectorate was established by the British in 1885. It was renamed the Niger Coast Protectorate in 1893 and in 1900 was joined to the Nigerian territories administered by the British government. Its name derives from the palm oil that was the chief product of the area.
subject: History

question: The Lagos colony and the Southern Protectorate was almagamated in _____.
solution: The protectorate was incorporated into the new Southern Nigeria Protectorate in February 1906, and Lagos became the capital of the Protectorate of Nigeria.
subject: History

question: Who was known as the Napoleon of West Africa?
solution: Described as African Napoleon, Samori Toure built a Muslim empire fighting off the French colonisation of West Africa in the 19th Century.
subject: History

question: What are the Kingmakers in Oyo called?
solution: The Oyomesi were the seven hereditary kingmakers in the empire headed by the Bashorun. Their duty was to install a new Alafin when the ruling one dies or to remove the Alafin. They also assisted the Alafin in the administration of the empire.
subject: History

question: Who brought Christianity to Liberia?
solution: Christianity reached Liberia in the 19th century with the arrival of freed slaves from the United States. Missionaries of various Protestant denominations started arriving in the 1820s, eventually forming what became one of the highest per capita missionary populations in the world.
subject: History

question: Who is the father of nationalism in Nigeria?
solution: Nigerian nationalism is territorial nationalism and emphasizes a cultural connection of the people to the land, particularly the Niger and the Benue Rivers. It first emerged in the 1920s under the influence of Herbert Macaulay, who is considered to be the founder of Nigerian nationalism.
subject: History

question: In what year did Britain invade Egypt?
solution: The British conquest of Egypt (1882), also known as Anglo-Egyptian War occurred in 1882 between Egyptian and Sudanese forces under Ahmed 'Urabi and the United Kingdom. It ended a nationalist uprising against the Khedive Tewfik Pasha. It established firm British influence over Egypt at the expense of the Egyptians, the French, and the Ottoman Empire, whose already weak authority became nominal.
subject: History

question: What is the king of the Nupe called?
solution: Etsu Nupe means King of Nupe
subject: History

question: Who founded Nupe?
solution: The Nupe kingdom was founded by Tsoede, alias Edegi who was born in 1465. The early Nupe history recognised Tsoede and his fundamental contributions to the building of Nupe dynasty.
subject: History

question: The Nigerian civil war lasted _____ months.
solution: The civil war lasted from 6 July 1967 to 15 January 1970 (2 years, 6 months/ 30 months)
subject: History

question: What country was the first to be introduced to Christianity in West Africa?
solution: Christian evangelism first took root in Western Africa when Methodists and Anglicans settled in Sierra Leone in 1804 to evangelize freed slaves returned to that British colony.
subject: History

question: The Nok culture was known for
solution: Nok culture is known for its unique terracotta sculptures and its early iron working. In Nigeria in 1943, a visitor came to archaeologist Bernard Fagg and drew his attention to some unique-looking artifacts, which Fagg and his colleagues eventually determined belonged to a then-unknown culture now known as the Nok.
subject: History

question: General Muhammadu Buhari regime was overthrown by
solution: The 1985 Nigerian coup d'état was a military coup which took place in Nigeria on 27 August 1985 when a faction of mid-level Armed Forces officers, led by the Chief of Army Staff Major-General Ibrahim Babangida, overthrew the government of Major General Muhammadu Buhari (who himself took power in the 1983 coup d'état).
subject: History

question: Who founded itsekiri kingdom?
solution: It is a well established fact that Oba Olua founded the Itsekiri kingdom for his unpopular son Iginua(1473- 1480A.D.)
subject: History

question: How many states were created by Gen. Sani Abacha?
solution: Gen. Sani Abacha created the following 6 states of Nigeria: Ebonyi; Bayelsa; Nasarawa; Zamfara; Gombe; Ekiti.
subject: History

question: Nigeria was birthed in the year
solution: On January 1, 1914, Lord Frederick Lugard, the governor of both the Northern Nigeria Protectorate and the Colony and Protectorate of Southern Nigeria, signed a document consolidating the two, thereby creating the Colony and Protectorate of Nigeria. Forty-six years later in 1960, Nigeria became an independent state.
subject: History

question: The Great Trek happened in what country?
solution: Great Trek, Afrikaans Groot Trek, the emigration of some 12,000 to 14,000 Boers from Cape Colony in South Africa between 1835 and the early 1840s, in rebellion against the policies of the British government and in search of fresh pasturelands.
subject: History

question: In what century did the Trans-Saharan trade start?
solution: Around the fifth century, thanks to the availability of the camel, Berber-speaking people began crossing the Sahara Desert. From the eighth century onward, annual trade caravans followed routes later described by Arabic authors with minute attention to detail.
subject: History

question: Who established the Tukulor empire?
solution: Tukulor empire, Tukulor also spelled Tukolor, or Toucouleur, Muslim theocracy that flourished in the 19th century in western Africa from Senegal eastward to Timbuktu (Tombouctou). The founder of the empire is Al-Ḥajj ʿUmar.
subject: History

question: Emperor Menelik II defeated Italian troops in the battle of
solution: The Battle of Adwa was the climactic battle of the First Italo-Ethiopian War. The Ethiopian forces defeated the Italian invading force.
subject: History

question: The Nigerian second republic began in the year ____
solution: The Second Nigerian Republic was a brief formation of the Nigerian state which succeeded the military governments formed after the overthrow of the first republic. It lasted from 1979-1983.
subject: History

question: The __________ were the first missionaries to arrive Nigeria.
solution: The Catholics, through the influence of the Portuguese traders, were the first missionaries to set foot on Nigerian soil. The primary objective of the early Christian missionaries was to convert the 'heathen' or the benighted African to Christianity via education.
subject: History

question: ________ preceded Nelson Mandela as president of South Africa.
solution: De Klerk was a South African politician who served as state president of South Africa from 1989 to 1994 and as deputy president from 1994 to 1996 in the democratic government. As South Africa's last head of state from the era of white-minority rule, he and his government dismantled the apartheid system and introduced universal suffrage. Ideologically a conservative and an economic liberal, he led the National Party (NP) from 1989 to 1997.
subject: History

question: What does the name Liberia mean?
solution: Settled in the early 1800s by freeborn Blacks and former slaves from America, Liberia, whose name means "land of freedom," has always struggled with its double cultural heritage: that of the settlers and of the indigenous Africans.
subject: History

question: The first coup in Nigeria was led by
solution: The 1966 Nigerian coup d'état began on 15 January 1966, when mutinous Nigerian soldiers led by Chukwuma Kaduna Nzeogwu and Emmanuel Ifeajuna killed 22 people including the Prime Minister of Nigeria, many senior politicians, many senior Army officers (including their wives), and sentinels on protective duty.
subject: History

question: In what year was ECOWAS established?
solution: The Economic Community Of West African States (ECOWAS) is a regional group of fifteen countries, founded in 1975.
subject: History

question: NYM(Nigerian Youth Movement) evolved from _____.
solution: The NYM was formed by Lagos nationalist led by Ernest Ikoli, H.O Davies, Dr. J.C Vaughan and Samuel Akinsanya in 1934. The party was first called Lagos Youth Movement by 1936 other Nigerian from other parts then changed to Nigerian Youth Movement.
subject: History

question: The __________ constitution introduced federalism in Nigeria.
solution: Bernard Bourdillon the Governor-general at that time initiated and laid the foundation of federalism in Nigeria in 1939 by creating three provinces. He later handed over the constitution to his successor Arthur Richards and it became the Richards Constitution of 1946.
subject: History

question: ________ moved the motion for Nigeria's independence.
solution: In 1953, Enahoro became the first to move the motion for Nigeria's independence which was eventually granted in 1960 after several political setbacks and defeats in parliament. Enahoro has been regarded by academics and many Nigerians as the "Father of Nigeria State".
subject: History

question: The Northern region of Nigeria gained self-government in ____.
solution: Western and Eastern Nigeria were both awarded self-government in 1957, while the Northern region would follow in 1959.
subject: History

question: In 1840, Sayyid Said transferred his capital from Muscat to?
solution: In 1840 Said moved his entire household to Zanzibar and declared it the new capital of his empire. Said and his many relatives and associates built numerous palaces, bath houses and country manors on Zanzibar, and introduced the commercial farming of cloves, sugar and other crops.
subject: History

question: Nigeria practised the Westminster system of government between
solution: The 'Westminster Model' existed in Nigeria from the time of independence in particular until the first military coup in 1966
subject: History

question: The equivalent of the Aare- ona kankanfo of the Yoruba pre-colonial system in the Hausa/Fulani pre-colonial system is the
solution: The Aare- ona kankanfo is the head of the army in the Yoruba pre-colonial system; the equivalent is the Madawaki who is the head of the army in the Hausa/Fulani pre-colonial system. The Waziri is the prime minister; the Galadima is head of the capital while the Dogari is the head of the police.
subject: History

question: The first British consul in Nigeria was
solution: In 1849, John Beecroft was appointed Consul of the Bights of Benin and Biafra by the British, a position he held (along with his governorship of Fernando Po) until his death in 1854
subject: History

question: The first almagamation in Nigeria happened in the year ___
solution: In 1906, the Southern protectorate was almagamated with the Lagos colony to form the colony and protectorate of Southern Nigeria.

question: Who was appointed by the federal government of Nigeria as the administrator of Western Region in 1962?
solution: He was appointed sole administrator of Western Region in June 1962 after a political crisis in the region, holding office in place of the Premier Samuel Akintola until December that year.
subject: History

question: The Coussey Commission Report laid the ground-work for the eventual independence of
solution: The recommendation of the Coussey Commission formed the basis of the 1950 constitution. It marked the beginning of the process of independence for the Gold Coast as Ghana, The first Sub -Saharan British Colony to achieve independence.

question: Kastina and Akwa-Ibom were created in September, _____.
solution: In September 1987 Babangida created Akwa Ibom State from part of Cross River State and Katsina State from part of Kaduna State.
subject: History

question: Nigeria joined the Organisation of Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) in__________
solution: It was on July 12, 1971, that Nigeria became the 11th Member of OPEC.

question: Nnamdi Azikiwe's West African Pilot was established in what year?
solution: The West African Pilot was a newspaper launched in Nigeria by Nnamdi Azikiwe in 1937, dedicated to fighting for independence from British colonial rule.
subject: History

question: When did Lagos become a colony?
solution: Lagos was bombarded by the British in 1851, annexed on 6 August 1861 and declared a colony on 5 March 1862.

question: The sokoto jihad began in what year
solution: The Fulani War of 1804–1808, also known as the Fulani Jihad or Jihad of Usman dan Fodio, was a military conflict in present-day Nigeria and Cameroon.
subject: History

question: The Kanem–Bornu Empire existed in areas which are now part of the following countries except
solution: The Kanem–Bornu Empire existed in areas which are now part of Niger, Cameroon, Central African Republic, Chad and Nigeria.
subject: History

question: The republic of Benin was formerly known as
solution: The History of Benin since the 16th century, for the geographical area included in 1960 in what was then called the Republic of Dahomey before becoming the People's Republic of Benin.
subject: History

question: Ismail Pasha was removed as the Khedive of Egypt at the behest of Great Britain in____________
solution: Isma'il Pasha was the Khedive of Egypt and conqueror of Sudan from 1863 to 1879, when he was removed at the behest of Great Britain.
subject: History

question: The United Nations was created as a result of the failure of the ________
solution:
The League of Nations was the first intergovernmental organization that was established after World War 1 in order to try and maintain peace. It was headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland and designed to be a forum for handling international disputes before they flared up into military action and caused domino effects that pulled ally nations into the conflict (as had happened with the Great War). Unfortunately, the League failed miserably in its intended goal: to prevent another world war from happening (WW2 broke out only two decades later).

The idea was for the League of Nations to prevent wars through disarmament, collective security, and negotiation. It was also involved in other issues such as drug trafficking, arms trade, and global health. Although the League disbanded during WW2, it was replaced with the United Nations, which is still going strong today.
subject: History

question: During the Great Trek, the Voortrekkers clashed with the indigenous Zulu people. Who was the Zulu king at that time?
solution: During the Great Trek, the Voortrekkers clashed with the indigenous Zulu people. The Zulu king at that time was Dingane kaSenzangakhona.
subject: History

question: Which European explorer is credited with discovering Sierra Leone in the 15th century?
solution: Vasco da Gama
subject: History

question: Which military leader introduced the "War Against Indiscipline" campaign in the 1980s?
solution: The 'War Against Indiscipline' campaign was introduced by General Muhammadu Buhari in March 1984. The campaign was aimed at promoting discipline, orderliness, and respect for public properties among Nigerians.
subject: History

question: The Richards Constitution of 1946 introduced a new political structure in Nigeria. What was a major criticism of this constitution by Nigerian nationalists?
solution: The Richards Constitution of 1946 was criticized by Nigerian nationalists because it failed to recognize the diversity of ethnic groups in Nigeria. The constitution was seen as a tool for the British to continue indirect rule and did not adequately represent the interests of the various ethnic groups in the country.

options.a: It failed to recognize the diversity of ethnic groups in Nigeria.
options.b: It favored the interests of urban elites over rural populations.
options.c: It restricted the activities of nationalist organizations.
options.d: It granted too much power to regional governments.

subject: History

question: The Lyttleton Constitution of 1954 introduced which significant political reform in Nigeria?
solution: The Lyttleton Constitution of 1954 introduced the federal system in Nigeria. This was a significant political reform as it decentralized power and allowed for regional governments to have more control over their affairs.

options.b: Creation of the Office of the Governor-General

question: The city of Ife, in present-day Nigeria, is famous for its artistic heritage, particularly its
solution: The city of Ife, in present-day Nigeria, is famous for its artistic heritage, particularly its Terracotta sculptures. These sculptures, often of human heads, are renowned for their naturalism and detail, and are considered among the highest achievements of African art.
subject: History

question: Who is often considered the founder of modern Egypt and the ruler of Egypt from 1805 to 1848?
solution: Mohammed Ali is often considered the founder of modern Egypt and was the ruler of Egypt from 1805 to 1848. He is known for his military and administrative reforms that laid the groundwork for the modernization of Egypt.

question: Which influential leader in the Sokoto Caliphate was responsible for administration and justice?
solution: In the Sokoto Caliphate, the Waziri was responsible for administration and justice. The Waziri was the vizier or minister of the Caliphate, and his duties included overseeing the administration of the state and ensuring justice was served.
subject: History

question: Which colonial policy was implemented to extract resources and labor from Nigeria for Britain's benefit?
solution: The colonial policy implemented by Britain in Nigeria was Indirect Rule. This system was introduced by Lord Lugard, the first Governor-General of Nigeria. Under this system, traditional rulers were allowed to govern their people as long as they followed British guidelines. This policy was primarily used to extract resources and labor from Nigeria for Britain's benefit.
subject: History

question: Which of the following was a significant trading commodity in the Nigeria area before 1800?
solution: Before 1800, Kola nuts were a significant trading commodity in the Nigeria area. They were used in social and religious ceremonies and also as a form of currency.
subject: History

question: The Omani Empire declined in the 19th century due to
solution: The decline of the Omani Empire in the 19th century was largely due to internal power struggles. The empire was weakened by a series of succession disputes and civil wars.
subject: History

question: Morocco is known for producing which luxury commodity, derived from the argan tree?
solution: Morocco is known for producing a luxury commodity derived from the argan tree, which is Argan oil. Argan oil is used in high-end cosmetic products and gourmet cooking, and is unique to the region of Morocco.
subject: History

question: Which pre-colonial kingdom in the Nigeria Area was renowned for its wealth and participation in the trans-Saharan trade?
solution: The Kanem-Bornu Empire, located in the Nigeria Area, was renowned for its wealth and participation in the trans-Saharan trade. The empire was a center of Islamic learning and trade, with connections stretching from North Africa to the Arabian Peninsula.
subject: History

question: Which major event led to the decline of the Benin Kingdom's influence in the 19th century?
solution: The decline of the Benin Kingdom's influence in the 19th century was majorly due to the Scramble for Africa. The Scramble for Africa was a period of rapid colonization of African territories by European powers in the late 19th century, which often led to the decline or destruction of existing African kingdoms and empires.
subject: History

question: The British colonial policy of "indirect rule" was first implemented in Nigeria by Lugard in which region?
solution: The British colonial policy of 'indirect rule' was first implemented in Nigeria by Lord Lugard in the Northern region, specifically in Kano. Indirect rule is a system of governance used by the British to control parts of their colonial empire, particularly in Africa and Asia, through pre-existing local power structures. These dependencies were often called 'protectorates' or 'trucial states'.
subject: History

question: The Aba Women's Riots of 1929 were a response to:
solution: The Aba Women's Riots of 1929, also known as the Women's War, was a response to the imposition of heavy taxes on women traders by the British colonial administration. The women protested against the Warrant Chiefs, who were representatives of the colonial government, and their oppressive taxation policies.

options.a: The prohibition of women from participating in politics.
options.b: The banning of traditional religious practices.
options.c: The imposition of heavy taxes on women traders.

subject: History

question: The Kanem-Borno Empire prospered due to its control over
solution: The Kanem-Borno Empire prospered due to its control over the salt trade. The empire was strategically located on trans-Saharan trade routes, and it capitalized on this by controlling the trade of salt, a valuable commodity at the time.
subject: History

question: Apartheid in South Africa was a system that institutionalized racial segregation and discrimination. What year did it officially begin?
solution: Apartheid officially began in South Africa in 1948. It was a system of institutionalized racial segregation and discrimination that lasted until the early 1990s. The National Party, which came to power in 1948, formalized and expanded segregationist laws into the apartheid system.
subject: History

question: The colonization of Liberia began in what year?
solution: The colonization of Liberia began in the year 1822. Liberia was founded by the American Colonization Society (ACS) as a place to send free African-Americans, who were freed from slavery but faced racial discrimination in the United States. The ACS believed black people would face better chances for full lives in Africa.
subject: History

question: What was the outcome of the Battle of Adwa in 1896?
solution: The outcome of the Battle of Adwa in 1896 was that Ethiopia gained full independence from Italy. The battle was a decisive victory for Ethiopia and marked the end of Italy's imperial ambitions in Africa.

options.a: Ethiopia and Italy signed a peace treaty.
options.c: Ethiopia gained full independence from Italy.

question: The ancient city of Carthage, once a powerful Phoenician civilization, was located in modern-day
solution: The ancient city of Carthage, once a powerful Phoenician civilization, was located in modern-day Tunisia. Carthage was a significant power in the Mediterranean and fought against Rome in the Punic Wars.
subject: History

question: Which organization played a crucial role in the fight against apartheid in South Africa and was led by Nelson Mandela?
solution: The African National Congress (ANC) played a crucial role in the fight against apartheid in South Africa. Nelson Mandela, who later became the first black president of South Africa, was a prominent leader of the ANC.

options.a: Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS)

subject: History

question: Who was the Oba of Benin during the height of the kingdom's power in the 19th century?
solution: Oba Ovonramwen was the Oba of Benin during the height of the kingdom's power in the 19th century. He was the ruler when the Punitive Expedition of 1897 occurred, in which the British exiled him and burned and looted the royal palace, marking the end of the Kingdom of Benin.
subject: History

question: The decline of the Songhai Empire in the 16th century was partly due to
solution: The decline of the Songhai Empire in the 16th century was due to a combination of internal unrest and external attacks, particularly from the Moroccan army. The other options are not historically accurate.

question: What was the major consequence of the Scramble for Africa in Yorubaland?
solution: The major consequence of the Scramble for Africa in Yorubaland was that Yorubaland lost its independence and became a British protectorate. The Scramble for Africa was a period of rapid colonization of African territories by European powers in the late 19th century. In the case of Yorubaland, it was the British who took control, thereby making it a British protectorate.

options.b: Yorubaland lost its independence and became a British protectorate.

question: Which conference, held in 1884-1885, laid the groundwork for the partition of Africa among European powers?
solution: The Conference of Berlin, also known as the Berlin Conference, was held from 1884 to 1885. It was organized by Otto von Bismarck, the first chancellor of Germany, and involved representatives from various European nations. The main aim of the conference was to divide Africa among the European powers, thereby laying the groundwork for the partition of Africa. This is a significant event in history as it led to the 'Scramble for Africa', where European powers rushed to claim African territories.
subject: History

question: Which African country experienced a prolonged and violent struggle for independence against French colonial rule, culminating in a war of independence that lasted from 1954 to 1962?
solution: Algeria experienced a prolonged and violent struggle for independence against French colonial rule, culminating in a war of independence that lasted from 1954 to 1962. The war ended with Algeria gaining its independence from France. This war is known for its guerrilla warfare and the use of torture by both sides, and it is considered one of the most important decolonization wars.

question: Which Yoruba city-state was the last to fall to British colonial rule in the late 19th century?
solution: Abeokuta was the last Yoruba city-state to fall to British colonial rule in the late 19th century. The British had already established control over other Yoruba city-states like Lagos, Oyo, and Ibadan. However, Abeokuta resisted British rule for a long time before finally succumbing to it.

question: How did the establishment of missionary schools during the colonial era affect the Nigerian economy?
solution: The establishment of missionary schools during the colonial era had a positive impact on the Nigerian economy. These schools improved the literacy rate and provided a skilled workforce. The education provided by these schools enabled Nigerians to take up administrative and professional roles, thereby contributing to economic development.

options.a: It led to increased government spending on education.
options.b: It hindered economic development due to religious conflicts.
options.c: It improved the literacy rate and provided a skilled workforce.
options.d: It favored British education over traditional Nigerian education.

subject: History

question: Lake Victoria, one of the largest freshwater lakes in the world, is shared by three countries. Which of the following countries is NOT among them?
solution: Lake Victoria is one of the largest freshwater lakes in the world and is shared by three countries: Uganda, Kenya, and Tanzania. Mozambique is not among the countries that share Lake Victoria.

question: Which British explorer is credited with the "discovery" of the Niger River, sparking further interest in the Nigeria Area?
solution: The British explorer credited with the 'discovery' of the Niger River is Mungo Park, but since his name is not in the options, none of the options is correct. question: Which Islamic reform movement aimed to purify the practice of Islam from local customs and traditions in West Africa during the 18th and 19th centuries?
solution: The Mahdist Movement was an Islamic reform movement that aimed to purify the practice of Islam from local customs and traditions in West Africa during the 18th and 19th centuries.
